source_metadata.title_raw: Victoria Loomis - A-18-210 - November 19, 2018 - Santa Cruz

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 19, 2018

Victoria Loomis
Deputy City Attorney
Atchison, Barisone & Condotti, APC
P.O. Box 481
Santa Cruz, CA 95061

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-18-210

Dear Ms. Loomis:

       This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Cruz Water Commissioner
James Mekis regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹
Please note that we are only providing advice under the Act. Also, please note that we are not a
finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we
provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts
underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

                                           QUESTION

       May Commissioner Mekis take part in Water Commission decisions to consider and
approve water rate increases for multi-family and commercial properties in Santa Cruz?

                                        CONCLUSION

       Commissioner Mekis has a conflict of interest in Water Commission decisions to increase
water rates. However, he may take part in the decisions so long as modified rates are applied
equally, proportionally, or by the same percentage to all multi-family and commercial properties in
the City.

                          FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR

       James Mekis serves as a Water Commissioner in the City of Santa Cruz. The Water
Commission is charged with making recommendations to City Council and has some decision-
making authority.

_______________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-210
Page No. 2

Commissioner Mekis owns a direct and an indirect interest in three real properties within the
City. He owns a 4.77-percent interest in a general partnership, Coronado Investment Group
("Coronado"). Coronado owns a 40-unit apartment building in the City as its only asset.
Commissioner Mekis receives a monthly statement, a quarterly check based on profits, and a K1 tax
form from Coronado. Additionally, Commissioner Mekis owns a 16.6-percent interest in a
commercial property in the City. His share in the commercial property is worth more than $2,000.
Commissioner Mekis also owns a single-family home in Santa Cruz with his spouse.

The Water Commission plans to consider whether to increase water rates for multi-family
and commercial properties in the City.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public
official's interests set forth in Section 87103. Specific to the provided facts, the following interests
are relevant to your request for advice:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• An interest in real property in which the officer has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(b).)

• An interest in any source of income to the official which aggregates to $500 or more
within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family. (Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal
finances.²

Based on the provided facts, Commissioner Mekis has a business interest in Coronado and a
separate business interest in a commercial property business, in which he is a 16.6-percent owner of
a commercial property. Commissioner Mekis also has real property interests in the commercial
property, in which he has a 16.6-percent interest,³ as well as the home he owns with his spouse. If

² While an official also has an interest in his or her personal finances, including those of an immediate family
member (Section 87103), an effect on an official's personal finances is not considered separately where the decision
affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property. (Regulation 18702.5(c)). Therefore, we do not analyze
Commissioner Mekis' interest in his personal finances.

³ Section 87103 provides: "For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment
or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a
business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater."

File No. A-18-210
Page No. 3

Commissioner Mekis receives $500 or more in income from either of his business interests, within 12 months prior to the Water Commission's decision to increase water rates, Commissioner Mekis also has source of income interests in these businesses. However, as a 4.77-percent owner of Coronado, Commissioner Mekis does not have separate interests in Coronado's apartment building or tenants of the building.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Standards for foreseeability vary depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision. An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a decision, and is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) Regulation 18701(a) establishes that a financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).

Regulation 18702.2(a)(3) provides the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on real property is material whenever the governmental decision "would impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to the parcel."

Accordingly, the financial effect of the Water Commission's decisions to modify water rates will have a reasonably foreseeable and material effect on Commissioner Mekis' property interest in the commercial property. Absent an applicable exception, Commissioner Mekis would have a disqualifying conflict of interest in the Water Commission's decisions regarding water rates.⁴

Public Generally

Section 87103 prohibits an official from taking part in a decision only if the effect of the decision of the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. Regulation 18703(a) establishes the general rule for determining when the decision's effect on the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.

"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment."

Additionally, Regulation 18703(c) provides specific rules for special circumstances where the public generally exception would apply. Relevant to this request for advice, Regulation 18703(e)(1) provides a specific rule in the following special circumstance:

_____________
⁴ We do not address the potential effect on each of Commissioner Mekis' interests. To the extent that the public generally exception applies, Commissioner Mekis is not disqualified from taking part in the decision based on any of the interests identified.

File No. A-18-210
Page No. 4

"The decision establishes or adjusts assessments, taxes, fees, or rates for
water, utility, or other broadly provided public services or facilities that are applied
equally, proportionally, or by the same percentage to the official's interest and other
businesses, properties, or individuals subject to the assessment, tax, fee, or rate."

In this case, the Water Commission's decisions to increase rates for multi-family and
commercial properties are decisions to adjust rates for water. So long as the Water Commission's
rate adjustments are applied equally, proportionally, or by the same percentage to all multi-family
and commercial properties subject to the rate, the public generally exception would apply and
Commissioner Mekis may take part in decisions related to water rates under Regulation
18703(e)(1).

Accordingly, Commissioner Mekis has a potential conflict of interest in Water Commission
decisions to consider increasing water rates. However, under Regulation 18703(e)(1), he may take
part in the decisions so long as the modified rates are applied equally, proportionally, or by the
same percentage to all multi-family and commercial properties subject to the rate.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Sara K. Purcelli
Counsel, Legal Division

SKP:jgl
